text: The laparoscopic left colectomy for colon cancer is performed using a percutaneous instrument. The sigmoid and descending colon are lifted upwards, exposing the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. Monopolar energy is used to open the peritoneum of the sigmoid colon, revealing the superior rectal artery. The inferior mesenteric artery is exposed at its origin, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the Toldt fascia both laterally and medially, reaching the root of the inferior mesenteric vein, which is clipped and divided at the tail of the pancreas. The tail of the pancreas is dissected from the transverse mesocolon, entering the lesser sac. Monopolar electrocautery is used to divide the lateral attachments of the sigmoid and descending colon, joining the medial dissection. The splenic flexure is mobilized by dividing the gastrocolic ligament, and the superior mesorectum is divided laterally on the left, posteriorly, and laterally on the right.

The surgeon interrupts the mesorectum using harmonic energy and divides the superior rectum with an endoscopic articulated linear stapler. A spatula is introduced into the vagina, and a posterior colpotomy is performed using monopolar electrocautery. The vagina is protected with a standard wound protector, and the specimen is extracted transvaginally.

The surgeon divides the mesentery of the descending colon and checks its perfusion using ICG fluorescence, confirming adequate blood flow at the proximal division. The descending colon is divided, and the anvil of a surgical circular stapler is introduced. The specimen is reintroduced into the abdominal cavity, and the wound protector is closed. A standard end-to-end circular anastomosis is performed, followed by an air leak test to ensure integrity. The vaginal protector is removed, and the colpotomy is closed with continuous suture transvaginally.
The final view of the patient's abdomen is shown after completing a laparoscopic left hemicolectomy. The percutaneous instruments' end effectors have been removed, leaving a clear surgical field.
